column131: >1_idBNS_SectionIPC_SectionChapterDescription
column132: >267c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f449Sec. 1 (1)-(6)S. 1 - S. 5Chapter I: PreliminaryThis section outlines the basic framework of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, including its official name, commencement date, and the geographical and jurisdictional areas where it applies. It determines who and what falls under its purview, such as Indian citizens and acts within India, setting the foundation for all subsequent provisions.
column133: >367c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f44aSec. 2 (1)-(39)S. 6 - S. 52AChapter I: PreliminaryThis section provides definitions for key terms used in the BNS, such as 'child' (under 18), 'gender' (including transgender), and 'movable property' (including intangible assets). It applies whenever legal interpretation requires clarity on these terms, ensuring consistent application across cases like theft or assault.
column134: >467c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f44bSec. 3 (1)-(9)S. 6, 7, 27, 32, 34, 35-38Chapter I: PreliminaryThis section offers general explanations for concepts within the BNS, such as joint liability or acts done with intent. It applies in situations where courts need to interpret how multiple offenders or specific circumstances affect liability, aiding in consistent judicial rulings.
column135: >567c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f44cSec. 4S. 53Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section details punishments under the BNS, introducing community service for six petty offences like minor theft. It applies when courts sentence offenders for small crimes, aiming for rehabilitation rather than incarceration, reflecting a modern approach to justice.
column136: >667c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f44dSec. 5S. 54 - S. 55AChapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section governs the commutation of sentences, allowing reductions or alterations based on factors like good behavior or health. It applies when authorities review a convict’s sentence, offering leniency in cases where continued punishment may be unjust or unnecessary.
column137: >767c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f44eSec. 6S. 57Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section standardizes fractions of punishment terms, reckoning life imprisonment as 20 years unless specified otherwise. It applies in sentencing to ensure clarity and consistency, particularly when calculating parole eligibility or partial terms for serious offences like murder.
column138: >867c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f44fSec. 7S. 60Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section allows courts to impose rigorous (with hard labor) or simple imprisonment, or a combination. It applies when tailoring punishment to the crime’s severity, such as rigorous terms for violent offences and simple terms for minor infractions, based on judicial discretion.
column139: >967c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f450Sec. 8 (1)-(7)S. 63 - S. 70Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section specifies fine amounts and consequences for non-payment, like additional imprisonment, with increased penalties. It applies when offenders fail to pay fines for crimes like theft or assault, ensuring financial penalties deter criminal behavior effectively.
column140: >1067c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f451Sec. 9 (1)-(2)S. 71Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section limits punishment for offences comprising multiple acts, capping it at the maximum for the gravest offence. It applies when a single act violates several laws, like theft and assault, preventing excessive sentencing for overlapping crimes.
column141: >1167c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f452Sec. 10S. 72Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section addresses cases where a person is guilty of one of several offences but doubt exists about which. It applies in ambiguous situations, like a fight causing unclear injury origins, allowing courts to impose a common punishment across possible offences.
column142: >1267c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f453Sec. 11S. 73Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section regulates solitary confinement, setting conditions and limits on its use. It applies to severe offences like murder where isolation is deemed necessary, ensuring it’s used humanely and sparingly to avoid excessive psychological harm to prisoners.
column143: >1367c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f454Sec. 12S. 74Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section imposes strict limits on solitary confinement duration, preventing prolonged isolation. It applies when courts sentence offenders to ensure confinement doesn’t exceed legal thresholds, protecting prisoners’ mental and physical well-being during punishment for serious crimes.
column144: >1467c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f455Sec. 13S. 75Chapter II: Of PunishmentsThis section enhances punishment for repeat offenders of specific crimes like theft or assault after prior convictions. It applies to habitual criminals, increasing penalties to deter recidivism and protect society from those who persist in criminal behavior.
column145: >1567c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f456Sec. 14S. 76Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section excuses acts done under legal obligation or mistaken belief of such duty due to a mistake of fact. It applies when someone, like a soldier, acts in good faith under orders or misapprehension, protecting them from liability.
column146: >1667c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f457Sec. 15S. 77Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section exempts judges from liability for acts done judicially within their jurisdiction. It applies when a judge makes a ruling, even if controversial, ensuring they can perform duties without fear of prosecution, provided they act in good faith.
column147: >1767c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f458Sec. 16S. 78Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section protects individuals acting under a court’s judgment or order, even if later deemed incorrect. It applies when someone enforces a court directive, like property seizure, shielding them from liability as long as they comply with the legal mandate.
column148: >1867c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f459Sec. 17S. 79Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section excuses acts justified by law or believed to be justified due to a mistake of fact. It applies when someone, like a security guard, uses force thinking it’s legal, offering a defense if their belief was reasonable and in good faith.
column149: >1967c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f45aSec. 18S. 80Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section exempts accidental harm during lawful acts performed with due care. It applies in cases like a surgeon unintentionally causing injury during surgery, ensuring no liability if the act was legal and precautions were taken.
column150: >2067c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f45bSec. 19S. 81Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section justifies acts causing harm to prevent greater harm, without criminal intent. It applies in emergencies, like breaking a dam to stop a flood, where the act is necessary to avert a larger disaster, known as the doctrine of necessity.
column151: >2167c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f45cSec. 20S. 82Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section states children under seven cannot be criminally liable due to lacking intent capacity. It applies when a young child commits an act like theft, ensuring they aren’t punished as their age prevents understanding the consequences of their actions.
column152: >2267c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f45dSec. 21S. 83Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section excuses children aged seven to twelve if they lack sufficient maturity to understand their actions. It applies when a child steals but doesn’t grasp the wrongness, offering a defense based on their developmental stage and comprehension.
column153: >2367c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f45eSec. 22S. 84Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section exempts acts by persons of unsound mind unable to understand their actions’ nature or wrongness. column154: >2467c8bccfdcd2f83aa7d0f45fSec. 23S. 85Chapter III: General ExceptionsThis section defends acts by someone involuntarily intoxicated, unable to judge their actions. column351: >21767c8c06cdcd2f83aa7d0f520Sec. 214S. 178Chapter IX: Offences Relating to Legal ProcessThis section punishes refusing to take an oath when required by a public servant. It applies when someone declines to swear in court, ensuring compliance with legal processes and penalties for obstructing judicial proceedings.
column352: >21867c8c06cdcd2f83aa7d0f521Sec. 215S. 179Chapter IX: Offences Relating to Legal ProcessThis section punishes refusing to answer a public servant’s authorized questions. It applies when someone stays silent during a police inquiry, ensuring accountability for impeding investigations by withholding responses to lawful queries.
column353: >21967c8c06cdcd2f83aa7d0f522Sec. 216S. 180Chapter IX: Offences Relating to Legal ProcessThis section punishes refusing to sign a statement when required by a public servant. It applies when someone won’t confirm their testimony to police, ensuring penalties for obstructing record accuracy and legal proceedings.
